one_line_takeaway: AttenMIA shows that white-box access to a transformer's per-layer, per-head self-attention matrices yields membership-inference and training-data-extraction signals that substantially and consistently outperform loss/perplexity/gradient-based MIA baselines (e.g. up to 0.996 AUC / 87.9% TPR@1%FPR on WikiMIA-32/LLaMA2-13B) and are largely unaffected by training-data deduplication.
key_finding: AttenMIA outperforms all tested baselines across models, datasets, and settings. On WikiMIA-32 with LLaMA-2-13B, AttenMIA(Perturbed) reaches 99.6 ROC AUC and 87.9% TPR@1%FPR, versus the strongest baseline RECALL at 92.2 AUC/13.3% TPR on the same setting (Table 2). Averaged across MIMIR's 7 domains and 3 Pythia scales (1.4B/2.8B/6.9B), AttenMIA(Perturbed) achieves 0.83-0.87 average ROC AUC versus RECALL's 0.54-0.56 and PETAL's 0.67 (Table 3); the paper reports this as +55% AUC over RECALL and +27% over PETAL, and an average TPR@1%FPR of 47.8% versus RECALL's 19.6% and PETAL's 13.3% (cited from Table 7, not shown in the excerpted pages). In a standalone feature-separability check on MIMIR-GitHub/Pythia-1.4B (Table 1), individual AttenMIA transitional-consistency features reach Hellinger Distance 0.66-0.70 between member/non-member score distributions, versus 0.506-0.557 for LiRA/LOSS/Zlib/Neighbor/Min-K%/Min-K%++. Deduplication (Pythia-dedup) barely changes attack performance (typically 0.00-0.03 AUC shift; e.g., AttenMIA stays at ~0.99 AUC on GitHub regardless of dedup), i.e. the standard mitigation of removing duplicate training text does not meaningfully reduce the attention-based signal (Table 5). Sequence length matters: shorter sequences (32 tokens) give much stronger signal than longer ones (128 tokens), with the drop especially pronounced in TPR@1%FPR. Layer analysis on Pythia-1.4B (PubMed Central subset) shows membership signal is distributed across all 24 layers rather than localized, with aggregating all layers (ROC AUC 0.954) beating the best single layer (0.862). In the extraction-ranking experiment (Table 6), the AttenMIA classifier score achieves Pearson r=0.480 with ROUGE-L similarity to ground truth, versus the best baseline (Zlib/XL ratio) at r=0.321, with several baselines (raw/lowercased/small-model perplexity) showing negative or near-zero correlation.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ATTENMIA, described as the first white-box membership inference attack (MIA) framework for LLMs that uses self-attention patterns rather than output-based signals (loss/perplexity) or gradients/hidden-state features. It defines two feature families: (1) 'transitional' features that quantify how attention matrices change across adjacent layers/heads (Consistency-Corr via correlation, Consistency-Frob via Frobenius distance, Consistency-KL via row-wise KL divergence, and barycenter-drift mean/variance capturing positional shift of attention mass), and (2) 'perturbation' features that measure the KL-divergence shift in a head's attention distribution when the input is perturbed via token dropping, token replacement, or non-member prefix insertion. These features are concatenated into a feature vector per sample and fed to a lightweight MLP classifier trained with 5-fold cross-validation to predict membership. The paper also builds a training-data-extraction pipeline that substitutes AttenMIA's classifier score for the ranking metrics used in Carlini et al.'s GPT-2 extraction protocol, and evaluates whether the AttenMIA signal correlates better with actual memorization (measured via ROUGE-L against ground truth) than baseline scoring functions.
threat_model: White-box adversary with full access to a released/open-weight LLM's internal state, specifically the attention weight matrices {A^(l,h)} for all layers l and heads h, given an arbitrary input sequence. The adversary does NOT have access to the original training data, optimizer state, or shadow models (attack is explicitly 'reference-free' / shadow-model-free, unlike LiRA-style attacks). No defenses (differential privacy, output perturbation, attention masking) are assumed to be in place. Goal: infer a binary membership label m(x) for a given sample, and, in the extended setting (Section 6), rank generated continuations by likelihood of being memorized training data to support automated training-data extraction from a public GPT-2 model.
limitations: The paper's threat model is strictly white-box (full access to per-layer, per-head attention matrices), so the attack does not apply to closed/API-only LLM deployments where internal attention weights are inaccessible; this constrains real-world applicability primarily to open-weight model auditing/release scenarios. Performance degrades markedly with sequence length (128-token sequences show substantially lower TPR@1%FPR than 32-token sequences across all models tested), meaning the attack is most potent on short excerpts. Evaluation is confined to open-weight base/pretrained models (Pythia, GPT-NeoX, OPT, LLaMA-2, GPT-2) without fine-tuning; no evaluation against instruction-tuned or RLHF'd deployed chat models is reported in the excerpted sections. The only defense tested is training-data deduplication, which the authors show is ineffective; the paper does not evaluate or propose a defense that does mitigate the attack (it calls for 'new defenses' as future work rather than delivering one). The extraction pipeline substitutes manual human verification of memorized text (used in the original Carlini et al. protocol) with an automated ROUGE-L proxy against ground-truth continuations, which is a methodological convenience the authors acknowledge changes the evaluation protocol from the original.

datasets_used: MIMIR (subsets: Wikipedia, GitHub, Pile CC, PubMed Central, arXiv, DM Math, HackerNews)
The Pile (used to train Pythia and Pythia-dedup)
Common Crawl-derived internet text prefixes (14,000 prefix-continuation pairs, for the GPT-2 data extraction experiment)
quantitative_results: WikiMIA-32, LLaMA2-13B: AttenMIA(Perturbed) 99.6 AUC / 87.9% TPR@1%FPR vs RECALL 92.2 AUC / 13.3% TPR@1%FPR
WikiMIA-32, average across 5 models: AttenMIA(Transitional) 99.0 AUC / 87.3% TPR vs RECALL 90.1 AUC / 17.5% TPR vs Min-K%++ 75.3 AUC / 6.6% TPR
MIMIR average (7 domains x 3 Pythia scales): AttenMIA(Perturbed) 0.83-0.87 AUC vs RECALL 0.54-0.56 AUC vs PETAL 0.67 AUC; reported as +55% over RECALL, +27% over PETAL
MIMIR average TPR@1%FPR (cited, Table 7 not in excerpt): AttenMIA 47.8% vs RECALL 19.6% vs PETAL 13.3%

Hellinger Distance, MIMIR-GitHub/Pythia-1.4B (Table 1): AttenMIA(Consistency-Corr) 0.704, (Consistency-KL) 0.701, (Consistency-Frob) 0.663 vs LiRA/Zlib 0.557, LOSS/Min-K% 0.532, Neighbor 0.545, Min-K%++ 0.506
Deduplication effect (Table 5, GitHub, Pythia 1.4b/2.8b/6.9b): AttenMIA(Perturbed) stays at 0.99/0.99/0.99 AUC (delta 0.00/0.00/-0.01); HackerNews stays ~0.70-0.71 AUC (delta -0.01 to 0.00)
Layer analysis, Pythia-1.4B/PubMed Central: best single layer 0.862 AUC vs all-24-layers-combined 0.954 AUC

Data extraction ranking correlation (Table 6): AttenMIA r=0.480 (Pearson, vs ROUGE-L) vs best baseline ratio_zlib_xl r=0.321; ppl_xl r=-0.206, ppl_lower r=-0.238, ppl_s r=-0.256 (negative correlations)
Extraction corpus: 14,000 GPT-2 generated prefix-continuation pairs; evaluation over top-1000/bottom-1000 ROUGE-L-ranked samples (2,000 total)
baselines_compared: Reference (Ref) attack / LiRA (Carlini et al., calibrated reference model)
RECALL (relative conditional log-likelihood shift, Xie et al.)
WS attack, RS attack, BT attack (word-substitution/random-substitution/back-translation style perturbation baselines, appear in Table 3)
